questions.question: What usually makes a Google senior design answer feel strong early?
options: It quickly clarifies scope and connects estimates to the design before diving into implementation detail.
It starts with every possible subsystem so the interviewer can see broad knowledge immediately.
It avoids assumptions until all edge cases are fully resolved.
It spends most of the time on one favorite storage technology.
questions.explanation: Strong answers earn trust early through structure, prioritization, and rough sizing.

questions.question: Why is a deliberate deep-dive choice important?
options: Because the best answer is to avoid any deep dive unless the interviewer insists on one.
Because depth shows judgment only when it is chosen to match the prompt's real risk or bottleneck.
Because every interview requires a database internals deep dive specifically.
Because deep dives work best when they happen accidentally during diagramming.
questions.explanation: The deep dive is strongest when it exposes the most important design tension, not random detail.

questions.question: Which opening most closely matches a strong Google interview pattern?
options: Start with assumptions, scope, and a quick workload estimate before naming the main components.
Draw a five-layer architecture immediately so the interviewer knows you are prepared.
List every non-functional requirement you can think of without prioritizing them.
Skip estimation because any numbers will probably be wrong.
questions.explanation: Interviewers usually prefer a scoped and workload-aware opening over a prebuilt diagram.

questions.question: What is the main risk of skipping rough sizing?
options: It makes the diagram harder to draw neatly.
It can leave shard counts, caching layers, and queue choices disconnected from the real workload.
It guarantees the interviewer will ask only API questions.
It mostly affects writing style rather than the actual architecture.
questions.explanation: Sizing anchors the architecture to reality and exposes which decisions matter.

questions.question: Which trade-off communication is strongest at senior level?
options: This gives us stronger consistency, but write latency and regional failover complexity both get worse.
This design has no real downside if we tune the cache carefully.
Trade-offs are mostly optional if the system is large enough.
I would mention trade-offs only if the interviewer explicitly asks.
questions.explanation: Senior answers make benefits and costs visible together.

questions.question: Which signal best shows operational maturity?
options: Naming a trendy database and saying it scales horizontally.
Explaining how a likely failure is detected, mitigated, and exposed through metrics or alerts.
Promising no downtime because the system is distributed.
Avoiding rollout discussion so the architecture stays clean.
questions.explanation: Operational credibility comes from realistic failure and observability thinking.

questions.question: What is the biggest danger of an accidental deep dive?
options: It often spends time on a technically interesting area that does not actually justify the design's main decisions.
It always lowers the estimated QPS by half.
It removes the need for redesign follow-ups.
It only matters in storage-heavy prompts.
questions.explanation: Depth matters only when it is applied to the most relevant risk or bottleneck.

questions.question: If the interviewer changes one requirement near the end, what is the strongest response?
options: Defend the original design and say the change would need a different interview.
State which assumption changed, which component or guarantee changes with it, and what new cost or risk appears.
Restart the whole answer from scratch without referencing the earlier design.
Ignore the changed constraint unless it affects the API surface.
questions.explanation: Strong redesign answers show constraint-aware adaptation, not panic or stubbornness.
